name: RABBIT-FILE-BUG-36
title: Parallel file-item.py invocations race on origin/bug-backlog-files; second push fails with 'incorrect old value provided'
description:
rabbit-file's branch_ops.py uses per-process worktree paths (.claude/tmp/bug-backlog-files-<pid>/) to isolate concurrent operations on the same filesystem path. This protects against worktree collision but does NOT protect against git ref races on the shared remote branch origin/bug-backlog-files.

REPRODUCTION (HEAD 46bc7ba8, observed 2026-05-25):
Two file-item.py invocations launched in parallel from a single Bash command (filing RABBIT-CAGE-BUG-105 and CONTRACT-BACKLOG-40 in one '&'-separated invocation). Both processes:
1. Set up their own per-pid worktree successfully (no path collision).
2. Both fetched origin/bug-backlog-files at the same revision.
3. Process A allocated its ID, committed, pushed → origin advanced.
4. Process B's allocate phase tried to fetch the updated ref but git rejected:
   error: fetching ref refs/remotes/origin/bug-backlog-files failed: incorrect old value provided
5. Process B failed with non-zero exit; process A succeeded.

ROOT CAUSE: branch_ops.allocate_id() and branch_ops.commit_item() each do a fetch+commit+push cycle. When two processes race, the second's fetch happens AFTER the first's push has updated the remote, but Git's atomic ref update sees the stale expected-old-value from the time of the fetch start and refuses. There's no retry loop, no lock, no serialization.

IMPACT:
- Any caller invoking file-item.py in parallel (e.g., a backstop filing multiple B/Bs at once) hits ~50% failure rate.
- Workaround: file serially. Acceptable for human-driven use; problematic for any automation that batches B/B filings.

POSSIBLE FIXES:
(a) Retry loop in branch_ops with exponential backoff — fetch + retry the operation on push rejection. Standard pattern for git-ref contention. Simple, robust, no new infrastructure.
(b) Per-feature lock file on the local filesystem (.claude/tmp/bug-backlog-files-feature-<feature>.lock) acquired before fetch — serializes ID allocation within a feature. Doesn't help cross-feature, but the ID allocation is per-feature so cross-feature parallel filings would benefit from no lock contention.
(c) Both (a) and (b): per-feature lock for local contention + retry loop for distributed contention (e.g., multiple machines or sessions sharing the same origin).

(a) alone is the smallest fix that solves the observed problem.

DETECTED BY: ws42 backstop session 2026-05-25 while filing RABBIT-CAGE-BUG-105 and CONTRACT-BACKLOG-40 in parallel as alongside findings from CONTRACT-BACKLOG-38 wave.

history.note: TDD cycle complete (session-override + superpowers TDD, cross-feature bundle wave)
